title: Roman script families
subtitle: A survey of the main Western script hands, from monumental capitals to humanist minuscule, and how they relate to each other
excerpt: Western calligraphy comprises a handful of major script families, each with a distinct historical moment and a distinct visual character. Roman capitals, uncial, Carolingian, gothic blackletter, humanist italic, and copperplate are the main branches. Understanding where each came from and how one grew out of another gives the calligrapher a map for choosing the right hand for any given commission.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906), chapter on historical survey of scripts. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Also drawn from: Alfred Fairbank, A Handwriting Manual (1932), public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: A capital or upper-case letter form. Monumental Roman capitals are entirely majuscule, there is no lower-case equivalent in the classical inscription tradition.
A small lower-case letter form. The development of minuscule scripts from the 4th century onwards allowed faster copying and more words per page than all-capital text.
The Italian Renaissance revival of Carolingian minuscule in the 15th century, refined into a rounded, open hand that became the direct ancestor of italic and of roman typefaces.

content.text: Western calligraphy runs in a continuous line from Roman inscription capitals of the ancient world to the copperplate hands of the Baroque era. Each major script grew out of what came before, simplified, elaborated, or reformed. The six families below are the chapters of that story.
Roman capitals
The monumental
majuscule

script was cut in stone with a broad chisel. It is the root of the Western alphabet. Trajan's Column (c. 113 AD) is the source of every roman typeface. Calligraphy based on it uses a broad-edge pen at about 30° to copy the thick-thin contrast. Roman capitals are formal and slow, best for headlines and dedication panels.
Uncial

Uncial script (4th-9th centuries) is a rounded book hand with a nearly flat nib angle, giving very shallow thick-thin contrast. Letters are large and open, and look like lower-case even at capital size: the forms a, d, e, g, h, m, n, q, and r are uncial inventions. These scripts are entirely
minuscule
in character. Half-uncial pushed the ascenders and descenders further, giving the first true lower-case.
Carolingian minuscule

Charlemagne's court scribes produced a clear, upright lower-case hand: Carolingian minuscule. It was the first hand to pair true lower-case letters with Roman capitals in one alphabet. Open counters and even spacing make it highly legible. Italian humanists later rediscovered it and called it the
humanist script
Gothic blackletter

As demand for books grew, scribes began to compress and angularise Carolingian forms. The result was blackletter: dense text with strong verticals and minimal curves. Gothic textura quadrata is the most formal type; cursiva and bastarda are quicker. Gothic is used today for headlines, certificates, and anything with a historical or Germanic look.
Italic

Italian humanist scribes revived Carolingian minuscule as an alternative to gothic. A faster, sloped version called chancery cursive was used in Renaissance courts. It was named italic after its Italian origin. Italic is the most versatile broad-edge hand: readable at small sizes and suited to formal and informal use. Most calligraphers start here.
Copperplate

Intaglio engraving allowed scribes to put their pen work into copper-engraved copy-books. The engraver's burin made hairlines and swelling shades that no broad-edge nib could match. A new class of pointed-flex nib was made to copy this effect in ink. Copperplate (also called English roundhand or Spencerian) is a pressure-and-release script: hairlines on the upstroke, shaded ovals on the down. It is the ancestor of modern wedding calligraphy.
Choosing a script for a commission
Monumental, historical, or institutional text: Roman capitals or Carolingian.
Medieval, religious, or Germanic register: Gothic blackletter.
General readable calligraphy, dedication panels, quotations: Italic.
Wedding stationery, envelope addressing, invitation suites: Copperplate or Spencerian.
